questions.question:
Based on the measurements recorded by the engineer, what is the spring constant ($k$) of this suspension component?

"A mechanical engineer is testing a suspension spring designed for a small drone. The spring has an initial, unstretched length ($L_0$) of $12.0\text{ cm}$. When a calibration weight of $25\text{ N}$ is suspended from the spring, it stretches to a new total length ($L$) of $16.5\text{ cm}$. The engineer needs to determine the spring constant ($k$) to ensure the drone's landing gear will respond predictably to different impact forces."

What is the total amount of work performed by the student to compress the spring to its armed position?

"A student is designing a vertical launcher for a $0.45\text{ kg}$ projectile. The launcher uses a heavy-duty spring with a spring constant ($k$) of $320\text{ N/m}$. To arm the device, the student compresses the spring exactly $0.12\text{ m}$ from its equilibrium position and locks it into place. The student calculates the work done to compress the spring, knowing that this work will be stored as elastic potential energy before the launch."

Based on the energy transfer observed during the test, what is the spring constant ($k$) of the recoil spring?

"Safety engineers are testing a new recoil spring for a cargo docking bay. A small motorized cart with a kinetic energy of $18.5\text{ J}$ is sent down a track to impact the spring. Upon impact, the spring compresses until the cart comes to a complete, momentary stop. At this exact point of maximum compression, all of the cart's kinetic energy has been transferred into the spring. Sensors record that the spring compressed exactly $0.24\text{ m}$."

How much additional work must be performed to stretch the spring from the $0.05\text{ m}$ position to the $0.15\text{ m}$ position?

"A lab technician is testing a high-precision spring used in an automated sorting arm. The spring has a known spring constant ($k$) of $480\text{ N/m}$. Initially, the sorting arm holds the spring in a pre-tensioned state, already stretched $0.05\text{ m}$ from its natural length. The arm then moves to its full extension, stretching the spring further until it is a total of $0.15\text{ m}$ from its natural length. The technician needs to calculate the additional work required to move the arm from the first position to the second."

Which material is stiffer, and what is its calculated spring constant ($k$)?

"A materials engineer is selecting a polymer for a vibration-dampening mount. She tests two different samples, Material A and Material B, by applying a series of loads and measuring the resulting compression.

Material A compresses by $1.4\text{ cm}$ when a load of $45\text{ N}$ is applied.
Material B compresses by $2.2\text{ cm}$ when a load of $65\text{ N}$ is applied.

The engineer defines 'stiffness' as the spring constant ($k$), where a higher value indicates a more rigid material that resists deformation more effectively."
options: Material A is stiffer, with a constant of $32.14\text{ N/cm}$
Material B is stiffer, with a constant of $29.55\text{ N/cm}$
Material A is stiffer, with a constant of $63.00\text{ N/cm}$
Material B is stiffer, with a constant of $143.00\text{ N/cm}$
questions.explanation: $k_A = 45 / 1.4 \approx 32.14\text{ N/cm}$. $k_B = 65 / 2.2 \approx 29.55\text{ N/cm}$. Material A is stiffer because it deforms less per unit of force applied.

questions.question:
Based on the experimental data provided, what is the stiffness ($k$) of the alloy wire?

"A laboratory technician is testing the stiffness of a new titanium-aluminum alloy wire. The wire is secured in a tensile tester that increases the pulling force while measuring the change in the wire's length. The technician records the following data points during the initial phase of the test:

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|}\hline \text{Force Applied (N)} & \text{Total Length of Wire (mm)} \\ \hline 0 & 500.0 \\ \hline 110 & 502.5 \\ \hline 220 & 505.0 \\ \hline 330 & 507.5 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

The technician needs to calculate the stiffness ($k$) of the wire in Newtons per millimeter ($\text{N/mm}$) to determine if it meets the specifications for a high-tension aerospace cable."
questions.explanation: Extension $\Delta L = 502.5 - 500.0 = 2.5\text{ mm}$. $k = F / \Delta L = 110 / 2.5 = 44.0\text{ N/mm}$. The slope is constant across all data points (e.g., $220/5.0 = 44.0$, $330/7.5 = 44.0$).

questions.question:
Which of the following correctly describes the work being performed on the cart by the resistive forces during the $4.2\text{ m}$ slide?

"A motorized supply cart with a mass of $85\text{ kg}$ is moving across a warehouse floor at a steady speed. The motor exerts a forward force of $120\text{ N}$ to maintain this motion. Suddenly, the operator sees an obstacle and shifts the motor into reverse-thrust mode, while simultaneously applying the mechanical brakes. During the braking phase, the cart continues to slide forward for $4.2\text{ m}$ before coming to a stop. During this distance, the total resistive force (braking + friction) acting against the motion is $310\text{ N}$."
options: $+504.0\text{ J}$, because the force is magnitude-positive
$+1,302.0\text{ J}$, because work is a scalar quantity and cannot be negative
$-1,302.0\text{ J}$, because the resistive force acts opposite to the direction of displacement
$-3,360.0\text{ J}$, because the work must be multiplied by the mass of the cart
questions.explanation: $W = F \times d = 310 \times 4.2 = 1,302\text{ J}$. Since the resistive force opposes the displacement, the work is negative: $-1,302.0\text{ J}$.

questions.question:
Which of the following correctly describes the work performed by gravity on the server rack during this $1.6\text{ m}$ lift?

"An IT technician is carefully lifting a heavy server rack with a mass of $32\text{ kg}$ to a higher shelf. The technician applies a steady upward force to lift the rack at a constant speed from the floor to a height of $1.6\text{ m}$. During this motion, two primary forces are acting on the rack: the upward pull of the technician and the downward pull of Earth's gravity. The technician wants to analyze the energy transfer (work) performed by each force individually."
questions.explanation: $F_g = mg = 32 \times 9.8 = 313.6\text{ N}$. $W = 313.6 \times 1.6 = 501.76\text{ J}$. Gravity acts downward while displacement is upward, so work is negative: $-501.76\text{ J}$.

questions.question:
Based on the data provided, what is the mechanical efficiency of this conveyor system?

"A logistics company is auditing the energy consumption of a heavy-duty conveyor belt used to move packages. The system's electric motor consumes $4,500\text{ J}$ of electrical energy to lift a batch of parcels from the loading dock to a shipping container. According to the sensors, the parcels (with a total mass of $160\text{ kg}$) are raised to a vertical height of $2.2\text{ m}$. The engineering team knows that the missing energy was lost as thermal energy due to friction in the motor and the belt rollers."

Based on the electrical energy consumed and the power output measured during the test, what is the energy efficiency of the pump?

"A municipality is testing a new electric pump designed to remove water from a flooded basement. The pump is rated at a power output of $850\text{ W}$. During a performance test, the pump runs for exactly $45\text{ s}$, drawing a total of $52,000\text{ J}$ of electrical energy from the power grid. The engineers need to determine how much of that electrical energy was successfully converted into mechanical power to move the water."

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered spring calculations, work sign conventions, and efficiency problems!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review the difference between total length and extension in spring problems.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on when work is negative and how to calculate work from spring compression.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: Hooke's Law, elastic potential energy, work sign conventions, and efficiency.
